C[ericosman: we're certainly playing in a passionate direction
C[ericosman: I was more worried about black L16 peep instead of M16 threat to cut
C[ericosman: white has dreams of using the extra lower right liberties afforded by the ""big eye"" to eventually attack the black right side group

C[ericosman: maybe the white d10 reduction should have either been c10 deeper invasion or peep at e9.  but I didn't like peep because I was hoping for maybe a wedge cut at e8 later

C[znordpol: didnt see you there, must ahve seen it somewhere and kept it at the back of my mind for ages,  no idea when or where
shusaku: u have 1 semi living group on the board. the key to making it live is J9
shusaku: life and death is a concept that emerges as a result of the rules of the game
shusaku: stones are captured when all of their liberties become completely surrounded by the opponents stones
shusaku: M1 is the final liberty for the group of 3 black stones At M3 M2 L2
shusaku: if W places a stone at M1 the black stones will be captured
shusaku: but if black plays M1 then W plays P1
shusaku: and the black stones are still captured no matter what happens
shusaku: the state of being in which the stones may never avoid inevitable capture means that stones are ""Dead""

shusaku: one of the rules of the game is that you may not play a stone anywhere in which there is no libertys for your stone to be alive. the only exception is that if by so playing in that space you capture your opponent to free up space for your stone
znordpol: so far I am aware of these things, but what does J9  do here?
shusaku: So when u play J9 it creates 1 space where it would be illegal for me to play.
shusaku: since u can only play 1 move per turn. every group needs a bare minimum of 2 spaces that would be suicide for the opponent to play in order to be alive
shusaku: at F10 you have 3 spaces
shusaku: but because i can play inside that i can reduce it down to 1
shusaku: so with J9 that makes what is called ""two eyes""
znordpol: it's all coming back to me now
C[shusaku: now that group in the middle is invincible and can never be captured
C[shusaku: at this stage the game is basically over
znordpol: so what now? I dont have any groups left to defend
shusaku: theres obviously alot of layers of strategy, tactics, shapes, life and death patterns
znordpol: so that's what losing at Go is like
shusaku: iv been playing a long time and i study the game
shusaku: theres a whole learning section on this site and even a whole collection of interactive life and death puzzles
znordpol: I've played sporadically and with ppl that knew less than I do
shusaku: and u can find opponents of all skill levels including bots for beginners to help you get started
shusaku: iv loved go for many years. And theres usually go tournaments wherever you may live
shusaku: are u from the US?
shusaku: Europe actually has alot of go clubs
znordpol: you got ties to Germany?
znordpol: Nice! I'm not much of a traveller myself
shusaku: Ich Sprechen  ein bisschen Deutsch, Ich bin von Amerika, aber ich habe eine Freunde in Flensburg
shusaku: Iv been to germany several times, mostly in Schleswig-Holstein and Hamburg.
znordpol: hey thats not too bad, did you learn just by visiting?
shusaku: over the years from my friend there and the few times i visited helped out alot
shusaku: my german vocabulary is not too big but i can get around and order my food attempt small talk XD
znordpol: that is true, I've been to the UK once, helped me a lot with my english
znordpol: just being there and talking to native speakers does so much
znordpol: I'm still quite unhappy with my spoken english, but I think my written english at least is quite passable

shusaku: but everything u say makes complete sense

shusaku: thats what gets me about german
shusaku: sometimes the way i speak german like to understand me u have to apply english grammar to the german words which i know makes no sense XD
shusaku: even like ""Ich Liebe Deutsch besuchen
shusaku: im trying to say I love visiting german
znordpol: I'm from the coast, but at the moment I'm in Thuringia
shusaku: just reversing the position of ""visiting"" and ""germany"" is something i dont know how to do in all cases
znordpol: yeah, I see how that gets non native speakers, but it's in no way making you incomprhensible
znordpol: ik incomprehensible isnt the right term here, but its the closest one I could think of just now

C[shusaku: thank you for the game
C[SiriusStarr: You too!  Please provide running feedback/commentary if you don't mind, 'cause like I said, I am basically a complete beginner.

C[shusaku: Sure! so first thing to go over will be how your approach these corners. The intention behind your move is to build a wall facing toward the outside of the corner. where my response at D5 defends the corner territory for white. :)

C[SiriusStarr: Not used to the large board, haha. So D6 would be a mistake since I don't really have any support to try to cut the chain. My thought was E6 makes D6 an actually viable move if you don't respond, to try to pen you to the bottom, and is preferable to  E4 because the wall will naturally help limit liberties if you extend that way.
SiriusStarr: (Like I said, I'm terrible, just a trying to talk through my thoughts so it's clear why I make mistakes rather than just making them.

shusaku: its ok this is helpful. In this case and almost all cases. One of the basic principles as a beginner to understand is your order of priorities on the board. In the opening generally we tell beginners to follow the rule ""Corner, side, center""

shusaku: Because the corners are the most efficient in terms of number of stones/moves it takes to control X amount of territory. The center requires 3x as many stones to occupy the same amount of points as the corners do and the sum of all the corners is about equal to the size of the entire center.
shusaku: There is an additional proverb. ""The first line = the line of death. the second line = the line of defeat, the third line = the line of territory, the 4th line = the line of influence""
C[shusaku: if you were to draw an imaginary boundary line right now whites stones are aiming to surround and take the territory from C1 - C6 to the edge of the board. a total of about 18 points.
shusaku: the influence of blacks stones radiates from the stones at E5 and E6 to the right hand side of the board. But dont accomplish anything else.

C[SiriusStarr: I suppose I'm unsure what the ""correct"" answer is.  To try to contest, or to create a threat elsewhere?  I suppose that's far too broad a question, because those sorts of macro-scale questions don't really have answers.

shusaku: in this case. learning the basic corner sequences and the elementary shapes I think is going to be good for you. I can link you some videos that cover these subjects to help you and of course ill outline through this game as well :)
C[SiriusStarr: Definitely, that would be helpful.
C[shusaku: So here this move is dual purpose. it solidifies whites terriitorial gain in the corner while also poking at the connection between blacks 2 Stones at E5 and 6 and the stone at D8
SiriusStarr: Yeah, I saw that coming, I just don't know enough about the game to know what else to do.  I'm essentially forced to play at E7 now to keep the chain from being cut.

C[shusaku: yes. So right now black is building up control over the center of the board. Your wall of stones radiates its power and influence to the right hand side out into the center of the board. This is called the ""direction"" of your stones. You will often hear the term ""direction of play"" in this case your direction of play with this group is toward the center
C[shusaku: now you stopped one cut. But because you cannot play 2 moves at once I will take the other cut that is available to me here

SiriusStarr: I don't much have a move besides E8 given the atari, and I have no other threats to make other than cutting at D6, but that's probably not worth sacrificing any hope of control over the top side.

shusaku: so in this case u protect your cut and i will take this stone and some control on the side as my compensation. if you want to keep your idea consistent then you can atari my stone by playing E9 which will force me to save my stone and then leave you with what is called ""sente"" this is a japanese word that literally means ""the initiative"" so you will have played a move that forces me to respond and then leaves you with the freedom to play anywhere on the board that you want to. And i would recommend that when trying to pick that move you filter down your order of priorities remembering what i told you earlier ""corner, side, center"" there are 2 completely open corners available on the board and those will be the biggest places available
shusaku: and yes the irony that sente in go translates to our eve alliance name is not lost on me XD
C[shusaku: C14 is the standard enclosure for a corner. We call it a knights move because the relationship between the 2 stones makes an L shape in the same way a knight moves in chess.
SiriusStarr: Yeah, I have a real tendency to just not play stones that aren't adjacent that I need to break.

C[shusaku: that's what every beginner does :) it's logical. and it's also the least efficient way to do anything at all :) once you understand basic shapes like that one there and how they work. your understanding will go up exponentially.
C[shusaku: have you ever heard of go magic?
shusaku: these guys are great. My go teacher makes course content for them
SiriusStarr: Heard of it but haven't looked into it at all yet.

C[shusaku: I really recommend you to check out their videos. they have so much there for beginners that can really help you out :) and in very digestible chunks that you could watch on a 30 minute lunch break
C[SiriusStarr:  Yeah, I'll check them out!

C[shusaku: so so far. you have played an extremely center oriented game. as an overall strategy. this is a viable way to play. we call it playing for influence. as you can see in the upper right and lower left. white has taken the territory toward the edges of the board and as compensation black is gaining influence facing toward the center of the board.
shusaku: btw. after you surprise your fiance with go skills XD please tell them I would be honored to play a game with them sometime. I'm curious how strong they are :)
SiriusStarr: I'm guessing you'll be disappointed, but we'll see, haha.  I don't believe they've ever formally studied anything, just played a bunch growing up, so.
SiriusStarr: So, though process here, my goal is to prevent white's stones from unifying in that corner.  The stone at O17 is pretty effectively trapped by O15/P16.
SiriusStarr: Playing M18 isn't advisable because white answering at M17 leads to any number of atari threats it seems like (forces a play at O16 by black, but then M18 can be threatened.
SiriusStarr: M17 provides more liberties to the iffy black stone at N17.  Frankly I don't know enough about the game to understand the implications of playing more aggressively at O18/P18/etc
C[shusaku: actually the thought process there is very sound.

C[shusaku: so here the position is essentially settled. there isn't any benefit for white to continue adding stones to the upper right given the value of other moves presently on the board. so now I return to our classic corner side center philosophy. and I'm going to combine it with another strategic concept. I have an enclosure in the lower right. that enclosure's direction of play radiates to the bottom side of the board. so an advantageous position is to have an extension placed off of my enclosure.
C[SiriusStarr:  Phaw, I have no answer to that.

C[shusaku: so what i was doing with that move was 2 things. moving into the area of potential being built by black to stop it from getting larger and reduce some of its point potential. and if u imagine an imaginary link between F10 and D12. Whites move severs that connection so now the E5 group is floating in the center of the board with no base making it vulnerable to attack so now when i push on this stone with G12 here im doing 2 things at once. Building more attack strength against your E5 group and also further eliminating the territorial potential black has built on that top
C[SiriusStarr: I guess I don't know how possibly to have defended against that...
SiriusStarr: I worry there is a sufficient skill gap here that I'm just losing without understanding why, if that makes sense.

C[shusaku: its ok. your not supposed to win the game. the purpose of this game is for me to just play simply. Evaluate your play. and then use the content of this game in order to make recommendations to you for how to improve. For instance. As i just mentioned the group at E5 is isolated and floating now. and you are now in a position where you are saying to yourself ""i dont know what to do about this""

shusaku: that in and of itself is a lesson. There is things that you can do about it. Stabilizing the group right now is the number 1 priority for black on this board and it all comes down to making a base for your group.

shusaku: in order for your stones to not eventually be captured there must be enough space for the group to make 2 eyes. and if you look at your group and try to imagine where those 2 eyes would possibly come from, they can only ever come from the intersections to the right hand side of your wall.

shusaku: J7 and K7 would both begin to establish a base for your wall. then you can start to envision the space in between that stone and your big wall as where your eyes are going to begin to come from :)

C[shusaku: once the game completes as well OGS has a full suite of teaching and review tools. so i can do things like make different variations for you to see and mark stones and highlight areas to illustrate things to help you understand them better :)

C[shusaku: a big part of learning go comes through playing as well to begin to develop a ""feel"" for the game. Alot of play is guided by intuition and feeling and you develop that over time by playing and observing games
C[SiriusStarr: Absolutely. Go is such a massive game (in terms it solution space) that it really can't be played fully consciously except in sort of limited scale engagements, it seems like.
C[shusaku: your whole board understanding is something that develops with time :)
C[SiriusStarr: Yeah, and it's something I have absolutely zero of since I've only really played smaller boards before.
C[shusaku: that's alright :) just the fact that your aware that it exists at all is a big leg up :)
C[SiriusStarr: Welp, I shouldn't play when tired, that was a huge throw.
C[shusaku: it's ok. but yes the move was a mistake. honestly I think with some simple shape knowledge and a little bit more game experience you will be able to develop your play significantly.
SiriusStarr: Board size is just too large for me to really be able to hold in my brain thus far.

C[shusaku: that's ok. improving at go is like exercising your physical muscles. it's ok that this board has too much going on for you to keep track of. the only point is for you to practice trying to hold as much as you can so that your capacity increases gradually. when we review. I will be zooming in on certain areas and focusing on local situations mostly :)
C[SiriusStarr: I don't win this one, lol
C[shusaku: haha. no. but there's a lot of material in this game that will be useful for you :)
C[SiriusStarr: Oh, absolutely, I was just speaking about that local fight
C[shusaku: everything's fine. we are entering the end game phase now :)
SiriusStarr: It's fine for you, haha
C[shusaku: hahaha. you did very well for a beginner overall honestly. I think you have strong potential for getting better if you keep at it :)
